summary: sensivity ships a Windows game-cheat loader with covert persistence, HWID exfiltration, and obfuscated control plane
details: The package distributes a Windows game-cheat/aimbot binary (sens.node, ~6.8MB, exporting symbols such as 'Teleport', 'LeftHand', 'LeftFoot', 'Head') alongside a heavily obfuscated server (server.obf.js) loaded via `eval(fs.readFileSync('server.obf.js','utf8'))` from launcher.js. On launch, launcher.js writes an HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run entry named 'OneDriveUpdate' pointing at a bundled OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs that silently re-launches `node launcher.js` with a hidden window, and sets `process.title='Runtime Broker'` to masquerade as a system process. cli.js invokes the VBS via wscript to bootstrap this loop. The obfuscated server collects a hardware fingerprint by reading HKLM MachineGuid via powershell, os.hostname, and the C: drive volume serial via WMIC/Get-Volume, SHA-256s them, and POSTs the HWID with a license key and nonce to a hardcoded remote LICENSE_URL hidden in the obfuscator.io string array. Execution of the cheat path is gated behind a polling check (`Get-Process chrome` looking for a window title matching 'YouTube'), an evasion trigger that suppresses the operator workflow inside sandboxes. `sens update` runs `taskkill /F /IM node.exe` and `taskkill /F /IM powershell.exe` against arbitrary user processes, then `npm i -g sensivity@latest`, allowing the publisher to swap in any future payload at the moving tag. The package self-describes only as a 'Mobile Control Panel'; none of the persistence, memory-manipulation, fingerprint exfiltration, or anti-analysis behavior is disclosed.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
